files.content:
AC-825IP micro SD card replacement
1. Power down the AC-825IP, disconnect the AC cord going to it and disconnect the backup battery if one
is installed.
2. Remove the 6 mounting screws (red arrows) on the AC-825IP main board, these are captive so they
should not fall out.
3. You may also remove the light bar at the top (green arrow) to use or just to get out of the way.
4. Lean the board out at the top, you should be able to lean it out enough without disconnecting the
wiring to the panel.
a. Be warned there are small cables plugged into the board on the back also so don't pull it out
too far.
1. The SD card is on the top left behind the plastic cover (blue arrow). There will be a small piece of clear
amber tape over it that must be removed.
2. The SD card does lock in with a click so push on it to release it and push it in until clicks to install.
1. Replace the 6 mounting screws (red arrows) on the AC-825IP main board, snug only do not
overtighten.
2. Replace the light bar if it was removed, line the white mark on the light bar up with the mark on the
cabinet in the center.
3. Reconnect the AC power and battery if equipped.

files.content:
The ultimate access
control platform
Secure. Flexible. Scalable
The Rosslare market-proven flagship networked control panel AC-825IP is well
positioned at the heart of Rosslare access control solutions. Optimized to meet
the full range of today's customer requirements, the controllers feature 6 to 58
reader configurations with a variety of expansions and a proven installed base
of more than one million doors across the globe.
Boasting sophisticated design and performance to fit projects of any size
and complexity, Rosslare controllers are ideal for new installations, as well as
replacement of existing systems. The controllers are comprehensive, modular
and versatile, and can be easily scaled to handle anything from a single facility
to multiple remote premises.
All controllers are equipped with professional enclosure, and are fully powered
by our QxControl Video management and access control platform as well
as AxTraxPro™ access control management platform that enables setting
AC-825IP
automated physical access control policies across facilities, perfect for
today’s most demanding users and organizations. QxControl and AxTraxPro™
Networked Control Panel can manage thousands of Rosslare networked control panels capable of
supporting hundreds of thousands of users, providing enhanced security with
excellent system scalability and performance.
Flexibility powered by security
The AC-825IP 6-door networked control panel communicates securely using AES High number of access points per single AC-825IP controller, supporting 12
256-bit encryption, with the management platform over TCP/IP connected to expansions via OSDP Secure Channel protocol
all access points, enabling implementation of the required access policy within
Offline Mode Operation – access control policy and door automation
a protected area. The AC-825IP features a wide range of expansions, making it
schedulers keep working even when network interruptions occur
ideal and cost-effective for projects of any size, from small to large or multi-site
Up to 16 credentials per user with a wide range of built-in access policy
with a high door count. The AC-825IP offers all the features of the QxControl
options included
and AxTraxPro™ platforms, with digital logic, non-volatile memory, and a backup
battery to continue implementing all access rules even even without a continuous Antipassback rules to prevent illegal access by door, by time or global
connection to the network or power.
Easy configuration of Access Point interlock rules allow the creation of simple
two-door "man traps" and highly customizable interlock scenarios in groups
Advanced communications including SNMP V3 and “Push” allow reduction of
network load, improved efficiency and higher security
Secure, lockable power-management enclosure with built-in illumination bar
Speed of installation – designed without compromise to make installers’
lives simpler while reducing installation time and complexity, allowing quick
troubleshooting and system maintenance
Power plug lock and power level status logging and monitoring using
AxTraxPro for AC Mains and UPS Battery operation

Key features and advantages

AC-825IP expansion 8 outputs
Designed with installers’ needs in mind, the AC-825IP has everything that professional
installers need in a scalable networked controller. State of the art bi-directional
push communication and expandability with plug and play expansions bring system
configuration flexibility to a new level. Equipped with a highly functional, stylish
enclosure with built-in illumination bar, smart cable management, split power supply
and many other features – the AC-825IP stands out from the crowd.
The AC-825IP is compatible with 4 types of expansion boards that communicate
over OSDP. Supports either 6 readers via OSDP or 4 Wiegand and 2 OSDP readers
simultaneously.

files.content:
Automatic Door Operation
• The first example is for an Automatic Door Operation to open a door on a
schedule.
• For the schedule I am using a time zone active from 8:00 AM until 4:00 PM
Monday through Friday. The steps to create this time zone are shown on the
next 4 slides.
• While this schedule is very simple, more complicated schedules may be
created with up to 16 time segments per day, 7 days per week plus holidays.
• Expand Timing by clicking on the + to the left.
• Select Time Zone then the Green + to create a new time zone.
• Left click and hold the mouse button at the beginning of the time period
(8:00) and drag the end (16:00).
• Change the name to “Weekdays”.

Internal technical support team (Josiah Jensen)

Can you help us set this up? Let me know if you need any additional information.

Thanks!
Josiah Jensen

Last Updated: May 1, 2026  
Audience: All Users  
Products: AxTraxNG, AxtraxPro, AC-Series Panels
Overview
If your access control panel won't appear in the software's c...
files.content:
Panel Not Connecting to Network
Last Updated: May 1, 2026  
Audience: All Users  
Products: AxTraxNG, AxtraxPro, AC-Series Panels
Overview
If your access control panel won't appear in the software's configuration, or you can't locate it on your network, this guide will help you diagnose and resolve the connection issue.
---
Symptoms
You're experiencing one or more of the following:
- Panel doesn't appear when searching in the software configuration
- Panel worked before but disappeared from the network
- Panel shows in the software list but won't respond to commands
- Network connectivity indicator shows as offline or disconnected
- Software simply won't locate the panel on the network
---
Common Causes
Panel Needs a Restart  
The panel has lost network synchronization and a simple power cycle restores connectivity. This is the most common fix.
Fresh Installation or Replacement Panel  
New or replacement panels sometimes need a restart or firmware update to establish network communication.
Network Changed by IT  
The network topology or IP addressing scheme was changed (common after IT infrastructure updates), and the panel hasn't reconnected yet.
Panel Moved Physically  
The panel was moved to a different location or subnet but the network configuration wasn't updated.
Firmware Not Installed  
New panels sometimes don't have firmware pre-installed and cannot communicate on the network until updated.
---
Solutions
Option 1: Power Cycle the Panel (Most Common Fix)
A simple restart resolves most connectivity issues.
Steps:
1. Locate the power supply connected to your panel
2. Unplug the power cable from the wall outlet or UPS
3. Wait 30 seconds (this ensures a full power drain)
4. Plug the power back in and wait for startup (typically 10-30 seconds)
5. Check the LED status light on the front of the panel (should be solid or slowly blinking green)
6. Try to locate the panel again in your software
What to expect: The panel will display a startup sequence. Once fully booted, the network connectivity should restore automatically.
---
Option 2: Test Panel Communication & Update Firmware if Needed
If a power cycle didn't work, test whether the panel can communicate with your software.
Steps:
1. In your access control software, go to Device Configuration or Panel Setup
2. Right-click on the panel and select Test or Verify Connection
3. If the test passes, the firmware is fine—move to Option 3
4. If the test fails, the panel cannot communicate and likely needs a firmware update:
- Connect the panel directly to your computer using an Ethernet cable (bypassing your network if possible)
   - Right-click the panel and select Firmware Update
   - Click OK and allow 3-5 minutes for the update to complete
- Once complete, the panel should restart and reconnect to your network
What to expect: A successful test means the panel can communicate. If firmware update was needed, the panel may go offline briefly during the update, then automatically reconnect.
---
Option 3: Verify Physical Connections & Basic Network Setup
Check the hardware and basic network configuration.
Steps:
1. Verify the Ethernet cable is connected:
- Confirm the cable is fully inserted into both the panel's network port and your computer/switch
- Look for a solid or blinking green light on the Ethernet port
- Try a different Ethernet cable if one is available
2. Check if your computer can see the panel's IP:
- In your software's Device Configuration, look for the panel's IP address
- Try to ping that address from Command Prompt: type `ping [panel-ip-address]`
- If ping works, the panel is reachable on your network
3. For AC-825 panels, check Windows Firewall settings:
- Go to Control Panel > Windows Defender Firewall > Advanced Settings
- Confirm that `AxtraxService.exe` has an inbound rule allowing connections
- If no rule exists, create one: allow all connections for `AxtraxService.exe`
4. If nothing above worked, the panel may be on a different network than your computer:
- This happens if the panel was moved to a different location or your IT department changed the network
- Contact your IT department to verify both the panel and computer are on the same network segment
- They may need to assign the panel a static IP address
---
Option 4: Contact Your IT Department
If the above steps didn't work, this is likely a network issue that requires IT support.
Information to provide to IT:
- Panel IP address (from your software's Device Configuration)
- Panel model number (e.g., AC-215, AC-425, AC-825IP)
- What changed recently (IT infrastructure updates, network changes, panel physically moved, etc.)
- Current status: "My panel won't connect to the software"
Common issues IT can resolve:
- Panel is on a different network subnet than your computer
- Network firewall rules are blocking communication
- Panel needs a static IP address assignment
- Network topology has changed since the panel was installed
---
Network Specifications (Reference)
For planning and tro

files.content:
Panel Not Connecting to Network
Last Updated: May 1, 2026
Audience: All Users
Products: AxTraxNG, AxtraxPro, AC-Series Panels
Overview
If your access control panel won't appear in the software's configuration, or you can't locate it on your network,
this guide will help you diagnose and resolve the connection issue.
---
Symptoms
You're experiencing one or more of the following:
- Panel doesn't appear when searching in the software configuration
- Panel worked before but disappeared from the network
- Panel shows in the software list but won't respond to commands
- Network connectivity indicator shows as offline or disconnected
- Software simply won't locate the panel on the network
---
Common Causes
Panel Needs a Restart
The panel has lost network synchronization and a simple power cycle restores connectivity. This is the most
common fix.
Fresh Installation or Replacement Panel
New or replacement panels sometimes need a restart or firmware update to establish network communication.
Network Changed by IT
The network topology or IP addressing scheme was changed (common after IT infrastructure updates), and the
panel hasn't reconnected yet.
Panel Moved Physically
The panel was moved to a different location or subnet but the network configuration wasn't updated.
Firmware Not Installed
New panels sometimes don't have firmware pre-installed and cannot communicate on the network until
updated.
---
Solutions
Option 1: Power Cycle the Panel (Most Common Fix)
A simple restart resolves most connectivity issues.
Steps:
1. **Locate the power supply** connected to your panel
2. **Unplug the power cable** from the wall outlet or UPS
3. **Wait 30 seconds** (this ensures a full power drain)
4. **Plug the power back in** and wait for startup (typically 10-30 seconds)
5. **Check the LED status light** on the front of the panel (should be solid or slowly blinking green)
6. **Try to locate the panel again** in your software
What to expect: The panel will display a startup sequence. Once fully booted, the network connectivity should
restore automatically.
---
Option 2: Test Panel Communication & Update Firmware if Needed
If a power cycle didn't work, test whether the panel can communicate with your software.
Steps:
1. **In your access control software**, go to **Device Configuration** or **Panel Setup**
2. **Right-click on the panel** and select **Test** or **Verify Connection**
3. **If the test passes**, the firmware is fine—move to Option 3
4. **If the test fails**, the panel cannot communicate and likely needs a firmware update:
- Connect the panel directly to your computer using an Ethernet cable (bypassing your network if possible)
- Right-click the panel and select **Firmware Update**
- Click **OK** and allow 3-5 minutes for the update to complete
- Once complete, the panel should restart and reconnect to your network
What to expect: A successful test means the panel can communicate. If firmware update was needed, the
panel may go offline briefly during the update, then automatically reconnect.
---

files.content:
How to Update Panel Firmware
Last Updated: April 30, 2026  
Audience: All Users  
Products: AxTraxNG, AxtraxPro, AC-Series Panels
Overview
Firmware updates add new features, improve stability, and are required before a panel can connect to your network. This guide walks you through the firmware update process step-by-step.
---
What Is Firmware?
Firmware is the software that runs on your access control panel. Think of it like the operating system on your computer—without it, the panel cannot function. Many newly shipped panels arrive without firmware pre-installed.
Signs your panel needs firmware:
- Panel shows "No firmware installed" in the software
- Firmware version displays as "0.0" or "None"
- Panel won't connect to the network despite correct cabling
- You receive a "Firmware update available" notification
---
Prerequisites
Before starting:
- Access to the control software (AxTraxNG 27.7.1.20 or AxtraxPro 28.0.5.2+) installed on your computer
- Physical access to the panel and power supply
- Ethernet cable to connect the panel to your computer (direct connection recommended for first-time updates)
- 15-30 minutes of uninterrupted time (do not interrupt the update process)
- Backup power or UPS (optional but recommended to prevent interruption if power is lost)
Supported Panel Models
Firmware updates are available for the following access control panels:
- AC-215 / AC-215IP — 2-door controller
- AC-225 / AC-225IP — 2-4 door controller (Series A and B)
- AC-425 / AC-425IP — 4-8 door controller (Series B)
- AC-825IP — 4-8 door controller with up to 12 expansion modules
Legacy Note: If you have a Series A AC-225 or AC-425 manufactured before 2011, contact support before updating—your panel may require special handling.
---
Update Process
Step 1: Prepare Your Setup
Best practice: Connect the panel directly to your computer with an Ethernet cable instead of through a network switch. This avoids network interruptions during the update.
1. Locate your panel and the Ethernet cable
2. Unplug the Ethernet cable from your panel (if currently connected)
3. Plug the Ethernet cable directly into your computer
4. Plug the other end directly into the panel's network port
5. Verify the panel is powered on (look for LED indicator on the front)
---
Step 2: Launch the Software
1. Open your access control software (AxTraxNG, AxtraxPro, etc.)
2. Go to the main configuration screen or "Device Management"
3. Look for a "Search" or "Add Device" button (exact name varies by software version)
4. Click to scan for available panels on your network
---
Step 3: Locate and Select Your Panel
1. Wait for the scan to complete (typically 10-20 seconds)
2. Your panel should appear in the list with a status or model number
3. Click to select your panel
4. Review the panel information:
- Model number
- Current firmware version (may show as "0.0" or "Not installed")
- Any available updates
---
Step 4: Start the Firmware Update
1. Right-click on the panel in the AC Networks tree on the left side of the software
2. Select "Firmware Update" from the context menu
3. A dialog will appear asking "Are you sure you want to update the firmware of the panel?"
4. Do NOT change any settings in this dialog—the panel type and firmware file should be pre-selected
5. Click "OK" to start the firmware download to the panel
If you see a warning: "Some panels have incompatible firmware. Are you sure you want to add them?" — Click "Yes" to proceed anyway. This is normal when updating older firmware versions.
---
Step 5: Monitor the Update Progress
Important: Do NOT disconnect power or unplug the Ethernet cable during this process.
1. After clicking OK, the dialog closes and you return to the main window
2. Select the Network in the left panel (under AC Networks)
3. Look at the "Downloads" column in the right panel—it will show progress like "452 / 472" (bytes downloaded)
4. The download typically takes 3-5 minutes depending on the firmware size
5. Watch the "Status" column—it should show "Download in progress" then change to complete
6. Check the Events log at the bottom for status messages like "Started Firmware update" and "Firmware update" events
7. Wait until the status shows the download is finished
Successful update indicators:
- Status changes from "Download in progress" to normal
- Events log shows "Firmware update" event with the panel name
- Downloads column stops incrementing
- Panel automatically restarts (you may see a brief connectivity interruption)
If you see errors in the Events log:
- "Invalid Firmware" — The firmware file may be corrupted or incompatible. Try the update again
- "Connection Lost" — Restart the panel and try again with a direct Ethernet connection
- "Wrong value is ac225v05_00_14" — Firmware version mismatch. Verify you're using the correct firmware for your panel model
---
Step 6: Verify the Update
1. Once the update completes, the panel will show a new firmware version (not "0.0")
2. Restart the panel (unplug and plug back in)

files.content:
How to Add, Manage, and Troubleshoot Access Cards and Credentials
Last Updated: April 30, 2026  
Audience: All Users  
Products: AxTraxNG, AxtraxPro, AC-Series Panels
Overview
This guide covers how to add new access cards to your system, manage credentials, and resolve common issues like cards not working or readers rejecting valid cards.
---
What Are Cards and Credentials?
Card: A physical credential (badge, key fob, card, or tag) that users swipe or tap on a reader to grant access.
Credential: The digital record in your software that associates a card ID with a user and their access permissions (which doors they can open, what times, etc.).
Reader: The device mounted near a door that reads the card and communicates with the panel.
---
Part 1: Adding a New Access Card
Step 1: Prepare the Card Information
Before you add a card, gather:
- Card ID number (often printed on the back of the card or key fob)
- User name (who the card belongs to)
- Card type (standard access card, biometric card, proximity card, etc.)
- Facility code (optional — identifies which facility this card is for, if using multiple locations)
- Access permissions (which doors they can open, time restrictions, etc.)
How to find the card ID:
- Many cards have the ID printed on the back (a long number)
- If not visible, you can "program" the card by tapping it on a reader in programming mode
- Check your card supplier documentation for the ID format
---
Step 2: Add the Card to the Software
1. Open your access control software (AxTraxNG, AxtraxPro, etc.)
2. Navigate to "Users," "Cards," or "Credentials" (varies by software version)
3. Click "Add New Card" or the "+" button
4. Fill in the card information:
- Card ID (the number on the card)
- User name
- Facility code (if required)
- Start and end dates (when the card is active)
- Access level or group (which doors they can open)
5. Click "Save" or "Add Card"
6. The software will display a confirmation message
---
Step 3: Sync with Panel
For the card to work, the panel must receive the new credential.
1. Select the panel from the device list in your software
2. Click "Upload Configuration," "Sync," or "Send to Panel"
3. Wait for the sync to complete (typically 10-30 seconds)
4. You should see a "Sync Complete" or "Success" message
---
Step 4: Test the Card
1. Go to the reader that corresponds to the door you gave access to
2. Swipe or tap the new card on the reader
3. The reader should beep or light up (behavior varies by reader type)
4. The door should unlock (if it's the right time and the credential is active)
5. If it doesn't work, go to Part 3: Troubleshooting
---
Part 2: Managing Existing Cards
Disabling a Card (Without Deleting)
If an employee leaves but you want to keep their record:
1. Go to Users > Cards or Credentials
2. Find the card in the list
3. Click to edit it
4. Change the "Status" to "Inactive" or "Disabled"
5. Set the "End Date" to today (optional but recommended)
6. Click "Save"
7. Sync the change to the panel (Upload Configuration)
The card will no longer grant access, but the record remains in your system.
Reassigning a Card to a New User
1. Go to Users > Cards or Credentials
2. Find the card
3. Click to edit it
4. Change the "User Name" to the new person
5. Update access permissions if needed
6. Click "Save"
7. Sync to the panel
The card is now assigned to the new user.
Updating Card Access Permissions
1. Go to Users > Cards
2. Find the card
3. Edit the "Access Group" or "Doors" field
4. Select which doors they should have access to
5. Set time restrictions if needed (e.g., "Only weekdays 8am-6pm")
6. Click "Save"
7. Sync to the panel
---
Part 3: Troubleshooting Card Issues
Issue: Card Won't Work After Adding It
Symptoms: Card was just added but doesn't grant access. Reader beeps but door doesn't unlock.
Cause: The configuration wasn't synced to the panel, or the card was added but not activated.
Fix:
1. Check the card status in software — it should show as "Active"
2. Verify the card end date hasn't already passed
3. Sync the configuration to the panel again (Upload Configuration / Sync)
4. Wait 30 seconds for the sync to reach the readers
5. Try the card again
---
Issue: New Codes Are Not Working
Symptoms: After adding new access codes in the software, the panel doesn't recognize them.
Cause: The panel firmware may need updating, or the synchronization failed.
Fix:
1. Verify the panel firmware is current (if showing version 0.0 or very old, update it first)
2. Check that the panel is connected to the network (see [Panel Connectivity](KB_01_Panel_Connectivity_Issues.md))
3. Manually resync the configuration:
- Select the panel
- Click "Upload Configuration" or "Sync"
- Wait for completion
4. If sync fails, check the error message:
   - "Panel Unreachable" — Panel lost network connection (restart it)
   - "Database Error" — Try syncing again, or restart your software
5. Try the card again after successful sync
---
Issue: Biometric Reader Sends Same ID for All Cards
Sympt

files.content:
Rosslare Security — North America
Technical Support Knowledge Base
Top 5 Support Issues — Resolution Guide
April 2026  |  Based on 2,000+ Support Tickets
Articles in This Document
Article 1: Cards / Credentials Not Working in AxTrax NG
Article 2: Panel Not Connecting in AxTrax NG
Article 3: How to Move AxTrax NG to a New Computer
Article 4: Standalone Controller Programming — Factory Reset, Code Enrollment, and Setup
Article 5: AxTrax NG — DB Disconnected or Client Not Connecting to Server
These articles address the five most frequently reported issues across the Rosslare North America support team from April 2025 through March 2026. Each article is written for installers and system administrators and can be published directly to a customer-facing knowledge base.
Troubleshooting: Cards / Credentials Not Working in AxTrax NG
Overview
If cardholders are presenting their credentials but the door is not unlocking, or if no event is being logged in AxTrax NG when a card is used, this article walks through the four most common causes and how to fix them. Most cases come down to the card not being in the system, the access group not including the correct reader or schedule, the panel being offline, or a Windows Firewall rule blocking communication.
Symptoms
Card is presented to a reader but the door does not unlock.
Reader shows a red LED or triple beep (access denied).
No event is logged in AxTrax NG when the card is used — this typically means the panel is offline.
Some cards work on a door while others do not.
Newly programmed cards are not working.
Common Causes and Solutions
Cause 1 — Card Is Not in the System
The most frequent reason cards fail. A card must be enrolled in AxTrax NG before it can be granted access at any reader.
Open AxTrax NG and go to the Cards section in the left navigation tree.
Search for the cardholder by name or card number.
If the card is NOT listed, you must add it. Go to Cards, click the green + (Add), and enter the card's facility code and card number. Click Save.
If the card IS listed, verify its Status is Active and it has not expired. An inactive or expired card will be denied at the reader even if the person exists in the system.
Cause 2 — Access Group Does Not Include the Reader or Is Outside the Time Schedule
Even if a card is in the system, it will be denied if its Access Group does not include the specific door being tested, or if the current time is outside the group's allowed schedule.
In AxTrax NG, go to Groups > Access Groups in the left tree.
Open the Access Group assigned to the cardholder.
Click the Readers tab. Confirm the door or reader where access is failing is listed and checked.
If the door is missing from the Access Group, add it. Also verify the Time Schedule allows access at the current time — a card can be blocked simply because it is outside the allowed hours.
Save the Access Group and test the card again.
Cause 3 — Panel Is Not Connected to the Software
If the panel managing the reader is offline, AxTrax NG cannot push access decisions. Cards will appear to be presented but no door relay will fire. A missing event log is the key indicator here.
In AxTrax NG, go to AC Networks in the left tree and expand the panel that the reader is connected to.
Check the panel's Status column in the Table View. 'Connected' or 'Initialized' means the panel is online. Any other status (red, grey, 'Disconnected') means the panel is offline.
If the panel is offline, resolve the panel connectivity issue first. See Article 2: Panel Not Connecting in AxTrax NG for full steps.
Once the panel is back online, re-test the card. Cards cannot be validated by an offline panel — the door relay will not fire.
Cause 4 — Windows Firewall or Antivirus Is Blocking Panel Communication
AxTrax NG communicates with panels over TCP port 10001. If this port is blocked, panels go offline and cards fail. Third-party antivirus products (McAfee, Symantec) are also a common cause — Rosslare has only tested and supports Windows Defender.
On the Windows PC running AxTrax NG, open Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security (Start > search for 'Windows Firewall with Advanced Security').
Click Inbound Rules on the left. Look for a rule named AxTraxNG or one allowing TCP port 10001.
If the rule is missing or shows as disabled, click New Rule > Port > TCP > Specific Local Ports: 10001 > Allow the Connection > apply to all profiles > name it AxTraxNG.
Also check if any third-party antivirus (McAfee, Symantec, etc.) is installed. Rosslare only supports Windows Defender. Third-party antivirus must be disabled or have exceptions configured for AxTrax NG.
Restart the AxTraxNG Server service (services.msc) and test again.
Still Having Issues?
Contact Rosslare Technical Support. Have ready: the card number and facility code, the panel model and IP address, a screenshot of the Access Group showing the reader assignment, and the AxTrax NG version (Help > About).
Related Articles
Article 2: Panel Not Connecting

powerful integration capabilities, now extended to KONE Office Flow Elevator
Destination Control. This integration enables seamless access and destination
control from the AxTraxPro™ platform, enhancing operational efficiency and
security within multistory buildings.

and benefits and software products

================================================================================
OPTION B: AUTOMATED SHAREPOINT LINKS
Azure App Registration for Direct File Access
================================================================================

WHAT THIS DOES:
- Generates shareable links for all files automatically
- Your search tool shows "📂 Open in SharePoint" buttons
- Team clicks → file opens directly from SharePoint
- No more navigating OneDrive manually
- Permanent setup - works forever once configured

FILES CREATED:
1. AZURE_SETUP_GUIDE.md
   → Step-by-step walkthrough (30 minutes)
   → You register an app in Azure AD
   → Get 3 credentials (copy & save)

2. generate_sharepoint_links.py
   → Runs after you have credentials
   → Generates links for all files
   → Updates search_index.json
   → Can re-run anytime

3. Updated search.html
   → Shows "Open in SharePoint" buttons when links exist
   → "Copy Link" feature for manual sharing
   → Falls back gracefully if no links available

QUICKSTART:
-----------
1. Open: AZURE_SETUP_GUIDE.md
2. Follow Steps 1-3 (register app, get credentials)
3. Run: python generate_sharepoint_links.py \
     --client-id "YOUR_ID" \
     --tenant-id "YOUR_ID" \
     --client-secret "YOUR_SECRET"
4. Share updated search_index.json with team
5. Done! Results now link directly to SharePoint

SETUP TIME:
-----------
- Azure registration: 10 minutes
- Running link generator: 5 minutes
- Total: ~15 minutes one-time setup

BENEFITS:
---------
✅ Fully automated - no manual link creation
✅ One-click file access for your team
✅ Permanent - doesn't expire
✅ Works with your existing search tool
✅ Can re-run anytime you add files
✅ Professional, enterprise-ready solution

WHEN TO RUN AGAIN:
------------------
After adding new files to documentation:
  python generate_sharepoint_links.py \
    --client-id "YOUR_ID" \
    --tenant-id "YOUR_ID" \
    --client-secret "YOUR_SECRET"

Then share the updated search_index.json with your team.

================================================================================
Ready to get started? Open AZURE_SETUP_GUIDE.md
================================================================================

╔═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║                    ROSSLARE DOCUMENTATION SEARCH                              ║
║                          USER QUICK START GUIDE                               ║
╚═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝

FINDING WHAT YOU NEED IN 3 STEPS:
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. OPEN THE SEARCH TOOL
   • Location: OneDrive → Rosslare Documentation folder
   • File: search.html
   • Action: Double-click to open in your browser


2. SEARCH FOR YOUR DOCUMENTATION
   Type keywords in the search box. A few examples:

   Looking for...                  Type...
   ───────────────────────────────────────
   AC-825 information              "AC-825"
   Installation instructions       "installation"
   Firmware updates               "firmware"
   Wiring diagrams                "wiring"
   Troubleshooting help           "troubleshooting"
   Configuration details          "configuration"
   Qxr documentation              "Qxr"
   AxtraxPro guides               "AxtraxPro"


3. OPEN THE RESULT YOU NEED
   • Search results appear instantly
   • Scroll through results
   • Click any result to open that document from OneDrive
   • Documents open in your default viewer (Adobe, Excel, etc.)


SEARCH TIPS:
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

✓ Space-separated keywords:    "AC-825 firmware installation"
                               (finds all three terms)

✓ Partial words work:          "config" finds "configuration", "configure"

✓ Case insensitive:            "ac-825" = "AC-825" = "Ac-825"

✓ Use more specific terms:     Searching "guide" might give too many results
                               Search "AC-825 firmware guide" instead

✓ Look at the snippet:         The gray box shows relevant content from file
                               Check if it's what you need before opening

✓ Check file type badge:       Shows format (PDF, Excel, HTML, etc.)


SEARCH OPTIONS:
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

□ Search content      (searches inside document text) ✓ ON
□ Search filenames    (searches document names)      ✓ ON


WHAT THE RESULTS SHOW:
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Result Item
├─ Filename:        AC-825-Firmware-v1.2.pdf
├─ File path:       📁 Hardware/Firmware/...
├─ File type badge: PDF, ✓ Content, ↗ Clickable
├─ Content preview: 📄 Excerpt showing why it matched your search
└─ Keywords:        Extracted terms from the document


COMMON SCENARIOS:
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Scenario: "I need AC-825 installation instructions"
  → Search: "AC-825 installation"
  → Look for: PDF or document with "installation" in the snippet
  → Click result to open


Scenario: "How do I troubleshoot a wiring issue?"
  → Search: "wiring troubleshooting"
  → Look for: Diagram or guide matching both terms
  → Check snippet to confirm it covers your issue


Scenario: "I need the latest firmware"
  → Search: "firmware"
  → Results sorted by relevance
  → Look for version number in filename
  → Click to download/view


Scenario: "Show me all AC-825 documentation"
  → Search: "AC-825"
  → All documents mentioning AC-825 appear
  → Browse through to find what you need


TROUBLESHOOTING:
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Problem: Nothing found for my search
  → Try simpler keywords: "AC-825" instead of "AC-825 firmware installation"
  → Try related terms: "install" instead of "installation"
  → The document might not be indexed yet - contact support

Problem: Too many results
  → Add more specific keywords: "AC-825 wiring diagram" instead of just "diagram"
  → Uncheck "Search content" to only search filenames

Problem: Document shows but can't open it
  → Verify OneDrive folder is synced on your computer
  → Try right-clicking result → Open in OneDrive
  → Ask your admin to re-sync the folder

Problem: Search tool loads but search box doesn't work
  → Try a different browser (Chrome, Firefox, Edge)
  → Hard refresh: Ctrl+F5 (Windows) or Cmd+Shift+R (Mac)
  → Close and reopen the file


SUPPORTED DOCUMENT TYPES:
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

✓ PDF              (.pdf)     - User guides, manuals, specifications
✓ Word             (.docx)    - Documentation, procedures, templates
✓ Excel            (.xlsx)    - Specs, compatibility matrices, comparisons
✓ HTML             (.html)    - Web-based guides, interactive content
✓ Images           (.png, jpg) - Diagrams, wiring layouts, schematics
✓ Text             (.txt)     - Configuration files, release notes


ACCESSING FROM DIFFERENT LOCATIONS:
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

From your computer:
  Open OneDrive →

 |  |  |  | 
Product Model ...
files.content:
=== Tamper Triggers ===

ROSSLARE TAMPER TRIGGERS - INTERNAL REFERENCE GUIDE |  |  |  | 
Technical Support Documentation - All Rosslare Access Control Products |  |  |  | 
 |  |  |  | 
Product Model | Tamper Trigger Type | Detection Method | Trigger Function | Configuration Required
AC-225 (2R/1D) | Door Monitor Contact (NC) | Held Open Detection | Triggers alarm when door remains open > configured timeout | Required for alarm functions
AC-225 (2R/1D) | Door Monitor Contact (NC) | Forced Open Detection | Immediate trigger on unauthorized door opening | Requires NC contact wiring
AC-225 (2R/1D) | REX Switch / Motion | Motion Activation | Detects unauthorized exit or motion in protected area | Optional - software configurable
AC-225 (2R/1D) | Reader Disconnection | Loss of Signal | Detects when reader cable is unplugged or damaged | Built-in - no config needed
AC-425 (1R/2D) | Door Monitor Contact (NC) | Held Open Detection | Triggers alarm when door remains open > configured timeout | Required for alarm functions
AC-425 (1R/2D) | Door Monitor Contact (NC) | Forced Open Detection | Immediate trigger on unauthorized door opening | Requires NC contact wiring
AC-425 (1R/2D) | REX Input (Configurable) | Exit Detection / Motion | Can be configured as REX or monitor input (not both) | Software selectable - see dipswitch settings
AC-425 (1R/2D) | Reader Disconnection | Loss of Signal | Detects when reader signal is lost | Built-in - no config needed
AC-825IP / D-805 | TAMP Wire (Violet/Blue) | Reader Tamper Detection | Direct reader tamper detection via OSDP protocol | Wired from reader pin (TAMP)
AC-825IP / D-805 | Door Monitor Contact (NC) | Held Open Detection | Triggers alarm when door remains open > configured timeout | Optional but recommended
AC-825IP / D-805 | Door Monitor Contact (NC) | Forced Open Detection | Immediate trigger on unauthorized door opening | Requires NC contact wiring
AC-825IP / D-805 | REX Switch / Motion | Motion/Exit Detection | Detects unauthorized exit attempts | Optional - software configurable
AC-825IP / D-805 | X-805 Tamper Switch | Enclosure Tamper (Expansion) | Detects physical tampering of expansion board enclosure | Only on ME-1505 expansion chassis
AC-825IP / D-805 | OSDP Communication | Bus Communication Failure | Loss of OSDP signal from reader or expansion boards | Built-in - auto-detected
AC-215U / AC-215IP | Door Monitor Contact (NC) | Held Open Detection | Triggers alarm when door remains open > configured timeout | Required for alarm functions
AC-215U / AC-215IP | Door Monitor Contact (NC) | Forced Open Detection | Immediate trigger on unauthorized door opening | Requires NC contact wiring
AC-215U / AC-215IP | REX Switch / Motion | Exit / Motion Detection | Detects unauthorized exit or motion | Optional - software configurable
AC-215U / AC-215IP | Reader Disconnection | Loss of Signal | Detects when reader cable is unplugged | Built-in - no config needed
AC-215U / AC-215IP | RS-485 Network Failure | Network Communication Loss | Loss of network connection to controller | Monitored automatically
ALL MODELS | Power Supply Tampering | Backup Battery / Aux Power | Detection of unauthorized tampering with power circuits | Optional monitoring
ALL MODELS | Firmware Integrity Check | System Memory Validation | Detection of unauthorized firmware modification | Built-in - automatic
ALL MODELS | Communication Interruption | Network/Serial Failure | Loss of data transmission to main panel or software | Monitored by system
 |  |  |  | 
 |  |  |  | 
IMPORTANT NOTES & CONFIGURATION REQUIREMENTS |  |  |  | 
1. Door Monitor Contact (NC) is REQUIRED for proper held-open and forced-open alarm detection |  |  |  | 
2. All AC-225 models default to 2 Reader / 1 Door configuration (see dipswitch SW3 for options) |  |  |  | 
3. AC-425 has only 1 input per reader/door; REX and Monitor inputs cannot be used simultaneously |  |  |  | 
4. AC-825IP supports direct reader tamper detection via OSDP TAMP wire (Violet/Blue connector) |  |  |  | 
5. X-805 expansion boards include tamper switch detection in ME-1505 chassis configurations |  |  |  | 
6. Reader disconnection is detected automatically when signal is lost (OSDP or Wiegand) |  |  |  | 
7. RS-485 network must be properly terminated (120 ohm resistors) for reliable tamper detection |  |  |  | 
8. REX Switch/Motion detector functionality is software-configurable in AxtraxNG settings |  |  |  | 
9. All tamper events should be logged and monitored through the access control software |  |  |  | 
10. Test tamper triggers during installation to verify proper configuration and response |  |  |  |

Installing AxtraxNG
Hardware
Technical Support Engineering
Rosslare Security NA
For more information please see
www.axtraxng.com
• The wiring shown in the next slide is an example of typical
internal wiring for 2 and 4 door panels. It is intended to show
the use of the internal power supply for lock power, follow all
local codes for lock wiring.
• The power supply on an AC Panel will supply up to 1.5 A at
13.8 VDC for locks, this is typically enough to power 2
maglocks or 4 electric strikes. For larger systems you must
supply power externally for the locks.
• There is no Fire alarm input on the Rosslare Access panels,
provisions must be made externally for Fire Alarm Door
release, as above, follow local code for wiring.

files.content:
Automations and ACLinks
Technical Support Engineering
Rosslare Security NA
For more information please see
www.axtraxng.com
• AC Links are used to create automations and perform custom operations on
access control panel. The simplest and most common use is to unlock a door
on a schedule.
• AC Links may also be used to augment the logic in a panel, to make use of
unused inputs or redirect inputs to perform a different function, i.e. use the
1A input for a motion detector request to exit.
• AC Links also provide a method for triggering relays to drive external alarms
for events such as door forced open or held open.
• AC Links may be used to trigger an action from a reader, a door bell is a
common application.

files.content:
Database Operations
(Backup/Restore/Move/Manage)
Technical Support Engineering
Rosslare Security NA
For more information please see
www.axtraxng.com
• Database management operations may be performed to insure the system
configuration information is not lost in the event of a PC hard drive failure, to
move a database from one machine to another or in the process of doing a
software upgrade.
• AxtraxNG utilizes Microsoft SQL Server Express to manage the database.
• AxtraxNG ver 24 supports operation with Microsoft SQL Server 2005,2008 and
2012.
• Microsoft SQL Server 2012 Express is installed by default with AxtraxNG ver
24, earlier versions utilized Microsoft SQL Server 2005 Express. Systems
upgraded from earlier versions of Axtrax or AxtraxNG may be running with
Microsoft SQL Server 2005 Express.
• AxtraxNG version 24 will import the following databases;
• AxtraxNG version 21.0 or later, database version 11 or later
(filename = ????????_Axtrax1_V11.BAK)
• Axtrax AS525 version 4.25, database version 23
(filename = Veritrax5_Veritrax_V23.BAK)
• For earlier versions contact Tech Support for conversion options.

files.content:
Installing AxtraxNG
Creating Networks and Finding
Panels
Technical Support Engineering
Rosslare Security NA
For more information please see
www.axtraxng.com
• This document will guide you through creating a network
and “finding” or connecting to an access control panel.
• The first example will be for a simple 1 panel serial network
utilizing the MD14 (U) cable directly from the PC to the
panel.
• The MD14 (U) cable provides an RS-232 to RS-485 interface
adapter in a cable form, the MD14U version combines a
USB to serial adapter and the RS485 converter hardware all
into a single cable assembly.
• The MD14(U) connects directly to the panel on the small
white 4 pin connector labeled MD14.
• Insure the RS-232/RS-485 switch is in the RS-485 position.

files.path: Josiah Notes/Access Control Card Format Training.docx
files.snippet:
Login and Initial Setup
Password issue resolved - caps lock was on from earlier license entry work
Standard password noted: Capital R-O-S-S-9876 for most systems
Mini PC password: Capital R, Zero, SSL...
files.content:
Login and Initial Setup
Password issue resolved - caps lock was on from earlier license entry work
Standard password noted: Capital R-O-S-S-9876 for most systems
Mini PC password: Capital R, Zero, SSLMAR3
Configuring Hexadecimal Card Display
Demonstrated how to enable hex view: Tools > Options > Cards Presentation > Hexadecimal
When enabled, cards display in hex format under Users > Cards tab
Current events continue to show in decimal, but new events display in hex
Understanding 26-Bit Wiegand Format
In 26-bit format, hex values can be concatenated directly to get the complete card number
Example shown: F27F4F represents the entire card contents in order
This format was used for a Florida client (A1)
Working with Different Card Bit Lengths
Challenge: Wiegand panels ignore cards of incorrect bit length entirely
Advantage: Mickey added feature that displays bit length in log when wrong format is used (e.g., "access denied, invalid card, 37 bit")
Solution: Enable secondary format on reader to test different bit lengths
Can enable multiple formats simultaneously to identify unknown cards
Card Format Flexibility Example
34-bit card can be presented different ways depending on data structure : 
8 bits + 24 bits = facility code 0-255 with large card number
16 bits + 16 bits = alternative valid format
Format selection depends on matching what's physically printed on the card
Creating Custom Card Formats
Custom formats can be created in the system settings
Example demonstrated: 26-bit format with no facility code : 
Structure: P (parity bit) + 8 F's or zeros (where facility code normally is) + 16 bits data + P (parity bit)
Notation: P = parity bit, F = facility code area, D = data
Parity bits can be cloned from standard 26-bit format but often not necessary
Trial and error approach works - experimenting with formats won't break anything
Historical Context: Wiegand Technology
Format originated in Northern Europe
Original method: magnetized steel wire strips glued to cards
26-bit was chosen because it was the maximum that could fit reliably on credit card-sized cards
Parity structure: first parity bit for first 13 bits, second for last 13 bits
Parity was crucial for early magnetic stripe readers but less important with modern RFID
Reference Resources
Multiple old card format reference files available on the system
RS2 (competitor) previously maintained excellent card format reference website
RS2 was acquired and website is no longer accessible
Site contained documentation for obscure formats and was regularly updated
Alternative reference site found but not as comprehensive as RS2

files.path: Josiah Notes/Access Control Panel Configuration Training.docx
files.snippet:
Panel Discovery and Network Setup
Panel discovery works by sending broadcast messages to devices with matching MAC addresses on the local LAN segment
Discovery only works on the same VLAN and subnet -...
files.content:
Panel Discovery and Network Setup
Panel discovery works by sending broadcast messages to devices with matching MAC addresses on the local LAN segment
Discovery only works on the same VLAN and subnet - will not work across VPNs or subnets due to broadcast traffic limitations
Two MAC address prefixes identify panel types: F8BC for older legacy panels and 0008DC for newer panels
New panels default to 192.168.1.1 subnet out of the box
Baud Rate Configuration
Default configuration requires manual adjustment - panels ship with mismatched settings that prevent communication
Network adapter defaults to 115 baud while DIP switches default to 9600 baud
Always set both to 9600 baud - higher speeds increase risk of firmware update failures
If firmware update fails at higher speeds, panel boots to bootloader which only supports 9600 baud, requiring reconfiguration anyway
The analogy used: "Sure you can get there faster if you go 150 miles an hour, but if you get pulled over, you're fucked"
Network Adapter Types and Legacy Panels
Legacy panel network adapter is an ethernet-to-serial converter module under the cover
Baud rate setting in software tells the network adapter what speed to use for serial communication to the controller board
Gateway types include MDIP32 (built-in modules on older panels) and MDN32 (external adapters or correct setting for new panels)
Critical: New panels with 0008 MAC addresses must stay set to MDN32 - changing this breaks communication
AC825 panels show different gateway type identification
Port Forwarding for Remote Management
Default port is 1000, but can be customized per panel
For remote management, use port forwarding with different ports per panel: port 1000 to panel 1, 2000 to panel 2, etc.
This allows multiple panels to appear at the same public IP address but be accessible via different ports
Most installations use daisy chaining instead to avoid running RS-485 wire across large buildings
Panel Addressing
Physical DIP switches on panel set the address
Address setting is necessary for daisy-chained panels but doesn't hurt anything on separate IP connections
Two reader per door configuration shows as "two little bumps" in the interface vs. one for single reader
Firmware Updates and Troubleshooting
Firmware is embedded within the Axtrax software, not downloaded from external sources
Firmware updates wipe panel memory and reload everything, even when pushing the same firmware version
Troubleshooting recommendation: For persistent weird issues (schedules not working, etc.), push a firmware update to completely reset the panel
Updates run faster at 115 baud (about 3 minutes for firmware plus database download)
Database download time varies: handful of users takes ~3.5 minutes, 1500 users takes significantly longer
Cumulex-specific note: Firmware update capability recently released, but availability of firmware files within software needs verification
Database Synchronization
Software only pushes users to panels where those users have access configured
Individual user additions push just that user to the panel
Troubleshooting trick: If users aren't working after adding, disable and re-enable the panel to force a full database push
This is equivalent to rebooting the panel
Panel Series Differences
B-series panels are the newer generation with blue PCB boards and black enclosures
Original panels have green boards with tan enclosures
Axtrax Pro does not differentiate between B-series and original panels in the interface
AC215 was the original bare board panel design
Event Filtering and Logging
Event filtering is configured at the panel level under Options > Event Filter
Default configuration minimizes event logging - outputs only report when opened from PC
Card reads are logged separately in the Access Events view, not Panel Events
All events count toward the 10,000 event database limit
Recommendation: For busy systems, disable non-critical event reporting (like door monitor contacts) to reduce database load
For troubleshooting schedules, enable door open/closed events to verify schedule execution
Door Configuration Options
Manual Door Operation: Controls whether door appears in manual operation list
Door Monitor Enabled: Shows door monitor contact status instead of lock relay status in software
Automatic Relock: Door relocks when monitor contact closes (door shuts) rather than waiting for timer - critical for strikes and motorized bolts
First Person Delay: On scheduled unlock, door only unlocks after first valid credential use after schedule start time
Door Open Time: Default 4 seconds
Extended Open Time: Per-user option for longer access time
Door Held Open Alarm: Default 30 seconds, configurable
Forced Open Alarm: Default immediate, can add delay to debounce wind-triggered false alarms
Door Alarms and Sounders
Alarms only generate event log entries by default - no automatic physical alarms
AC Links required to trigger panel sounder or external devices
Panel sounder has different tones: shor

files.snippet:
Key Fobs & Credentials
EM (125 kHz) Fobs
Two shapes available: rectangular (TB/TL) and round
Currently only stocked in black; previously available in blue and gray
These have the worst read range of a...
files.content:
Key Fobs & Credentials
EM (125 kHz) Fobs
Two shapes available: rectangular (TB/TL) and round
Currently only stocked in black; previously available in blue and gray
These have the worst read range of all fobs but are popular due to smaller size
Rectangular fobs are less durable and break easily, especially on key rings
Performance issue: placing on key ring introduces metal into the antenna coil, significantly detuning the RF signal
Mifare Fobs
Sharp tooth (rounded triangle) shape is the standard Mifare fob
Until recently, all Mifare fobs were this shape
A larger rectangular Mifare fob exists but few have been distributed
Multiple flavors of Mifare fobs available
Third-Party Credentials
Some distributors (notably one in Florida) import generic fobs from China with full 24-bit numbers printed instead of split facility code/ID format
Farpoint credentials are currently resold; hoping to replace with Rossler solutions
Bluetooth Credentials & Licensing
Farpoint's Bluetooth implementation is easier from user standpoint
Farpoint license tied to phone MAC address - must purchase new license if phone is lost or upgraded
Rossler's Bluetooth credentials are provided at no charge to end users
This creates dealer resistance as they cannot profit from credential sales
Card Formats & Facility Codes
26-bit Format Standards
Standard format: 8-bit facility code + 16-bit ID
Some international cards print entire 24-bit value instead of split format
HID cards contain facility codes but don't print them on cards (security measure)
Handling Missing Facility Codes
For takeovers without facility code information, can validate only 16-bit ID portion
Mercury/Cumulex: Use "Wiegand Zero" setting to ignore facility codes
Axtrax: Create custom format called "26-bit no facility code"
Essential for sites with multiple facility codes from different card orders
Troubleshooting Card Numbers
Use hexadecimal display option in Axtrax event log to diagnose card format issues
Method: Read card in hex, convert to appropriate bit format, match to printed number
Axtrax Software & Licensing
Axtrax NG
Free up to 256 reader ports (any reader port counts whether in use or not)
Example: AT-825 has 6 ports (2 OSDP + 4 Wiegand) all count toward limit
Level 1 and Level 2 licenses exist for larger deployments, mostly school districts
Originally used USB dongle, now uses hardware ID
Critical: NG hardware ID is different from Pro hardware ID - must use correct tool for each
Only China office can create NG licenses; cannot be generated independently
Axtrax Pro
Licensing done via server in Israel
Same reader port counting methodology as NG
Video Integration
Hikvision/Dahua Integration
Requires NVR and camera-based licensing
Available license levels (not fully documented in price list): 
L1: 2 cameras, 1 NVR
L5: Unlimited cameras, up to 5 NVRs
Part numbers: AX-HIC for Hikvision, AX-DAU for Dahua
Functionality unreliable over time due to firmware updates
Enables live view or snapshot pop-ups on card swipes or alarms
Can trigger camera views on events like doors held open too long
Few Dahua integrations sold; most experience is with Hikvision
Biometric Readers
Discontinued: B8000 Series & 9350
B8000 series: Discontinued, poor quality, 2-year warranty expired on existing units
AY-9350 (facial recognition): EOL'd two years ago 
Major limitation: Only stored one reference image per user
Failed in multi-reader installations with different lighting conditions
Current: 9000 Series
All models include Bluetooth support using Rossler app
AY-9120: Fingerprint + EM cards + Bluetooth
AY-9150: Fingerprint + Mifare (CSN mode) + Bluetooth
AY-9250: Fingerprint + EM or Mifare + Bluetooth + built-in snapshot camera 
Camera stores images locally when card/fingerprint used
Higher price point limits adoption
Images cannot trigger live pop-ups (stored only)
General Notes
Biometric readers are AY (reader only), not standalone controllers
Not outdoor rated
Moderate lifespan even in indoor installations
Discontinued Products
AX Time
Time and attendance module
Still appears in installer but no longer available
Part number: AX-TM
Could not handle time frames spanning midnight
Predated Cenarion acquisition
If customers inquire: Not available, cannot issue licenses, refer complex issues to Mickey
Vitrax (V-Tracks)
Third-party PC-based NVR with pop-up integration
Required separate license purchase
Performance issues: Extremely slow even on high-spec hardware (i7, SSD, 16GB RAM)
Pop-up delays made system unusable for real-time monitoring
No longer sold
Other Readers
AY-C and AY-D series: Indoor-only, inexpensive readers - not recommended
E-series (AY-E12, AY-E20): Ultra-slim readers with aesthetic bezels 
Do not work well on metal door frames due to proximity to metal
E-series keypads have piezo pressure sensitivity issues causing multi-key triggering
Not recommended
AY-E20: Could output Wiegand, Serial, or Clock-and-Data - discontinued years ago
O2S Secure Readers
Rossler's answer to HID iClass
U

files.path: Josiah Notes/Axtrax Pro Licensing Overview March 10, 2026.docx
files.snippet:
Licensing Model
Most Axtrax licensing is one-time fee, with the exception of the new Axtrax Pro Secure feature
Axtrax Pro Secure uses a subscription model priced per operator (not per user) at approxi...
files.content:
Licensing Model
Most Axtrax licensing is one-time fee, with the exception of the new Axtrax Pro Secure feature
Axtrax Pro Secure uses a subscription model priced per operator (not per user) at approximately $5-$10/month, billed annually
Dealers and distributors will receive a portion of the annual subscription fee as recurring revenue
Axtrax Pro Secure Feature
Includes a secured connection option that uses a VPN connection built into the Axtrax Pro software itself
The VPN connects to a secure proxy server/portal in the cloud, which then connects to the local system via VPN
Users log into the cloud portal (via SSL or similar), which maintains the VPN connection to the local system
The subscription management is handled by the cloud proxy, not the local server
The remote secure operators option must be enabled in the Axtrax Pro license, but the subscription is purchased separately
License Management
Licenses are looked up by hardware ID
License files are generated and sent to customers
Additional features (access ports, web clients) can be added to existing licenses
Access Point Licensing Optimization
Customers should minimize the total number of license items to reduce costs
Example: For 44 licenses, buying one 32-license and one 16-license package may be cheaper than buying 32 + 8 + 4, even with unused licenses
Sales teams may not always optimize these configurations
Integration License Options
All of the following are one-time enable/disable licenses (not quantity-based):
HIC Vision Biometrics: Enables support for Hick Vision biometric terminals
Idemia Biometrics: Enables support for Idemia biometric terminals
Milestone X Protect: Interfaces with Milestone video surveillance system, enables pop-ups and user management from Milestone
Bass IP Intercom: Cloud-based intercom system for apartment buildings, allows phone-based intercom calls; developed with funding from SAS
Coney Elevators/Destination Control: Connects to Coney smart controllers over network for elevator control, allows user synchronization between systems
STID Biometrics: Another biometric reader option
LDAP: Enables connection to LDAP servers to download cardholder information (not operators); requires backend configuration to fully populate credential data
Attendance Report: Time and attendance feature (details unclear)

files.snippet:
Used Equipment Support Policy
Company does not support used equipment purchased from unauthorized sources (eBay, resellers)
Policy must be communicated clearly and firmly to customers, especially repe...
files.content:
Used Equipment Support Policy
Company does not support used equipment purchased from unauthorized sources (eBay, resellers)
Policy must be communicated clearly and firmly to customers, especially repeat offenders
Discontinued products like mini PCs are explicitly not supported regardless of source
Warranty status and purchase through authorized distribution are key factors
Specific case: Phil at High Tech (installer in New York) repeatedly purchases used equipment and requests support - has been denied
Firmware Management
825 Panel Firmware:
825 panels ship with NG firmware by default
Cumulex requires pro firmware on 825 panels
Pro firmware can now be pushed through Cumulex as of last Thursday
Previous workflow required pre-flashing panels before shipment
Keep pro-firmware panels in stock for Cumulex demos and deployments
Firmware Rollback Process:
Rolling back from pro to NG firmware on 825s is complex and error-prone
Requires specific dip switch patterns and timing
Must power up, wait 60-90 seconds, flip dip switch 1 off while powered to erase firmware
Process has high failure rate - boards can brick even when following instructions correctly
Legacy panels can roll back firmware without issues
Encryption and security features in pro firmware prevent easy rollback
Future Considerations:
825s may start shipping with pro firmware after end of year (unannounced change expected)
This will create challenges for customers wanting to use NG
Sample and Demo Equipment
Significant equipment cost per demo kit: approximately $500 including 825 ($250-300), readers ($25-30 each), and accessories
Concern about ROI on demo equipment - many dealers receive demos but don't activate or use them
Approximately half of Cumulex demo boxes sent to dealers are offline or never came online
SQL Database Management
Common Issues:
Event history grows indefinitely by default in NG
Databases can grow to 10+ GB with event data dating back to mid-2000s
SQL Server Express has hard limit of 10,000 records per table
When event table exceeds limits, various failures occur
Event Storage Limits:
Current limit in software: maximum 100 days
No clear reason for 100-day limit - should ideally support at least one year
Large systems (e.g., Loretto School District with 1000+ doors) limit to 60-90 days to prevent database bloat
Database Troubleshooting Tools:
SQL Server Studio (full-featured, heavyweight)
SQL Query Plus (lightweight, requires .NET Framework)
Scripts available in SQL tools directory for common fixes
Script to check table sizes and identify full tables
Script to delete old events and messages
Password reset script for operator accounts
Database Restoration:
Clear database (empty template) used for new installations
Wiping database restores the clear database, removing all data
Directory structure from original backup can cause restoration failures
Multiple SQL versions in use (2012, 2019, 2022) - databases cannot be restored to older versions
Password Encryption:
Passwords encrypted in database starting with version 27.7.1.18 or .19
Upgrade process encrypts passwords but may not fix username capitalization
Administrator username must have capital 'A' to match encrypted format
Only way to reset passwords is through SQL script
Panel Deletion Behavior:
Deleting panel from database removes all references across all tables
Clears from access groups, AC links, and all associations
Access group structure and time zones remain, but linkages to panel are removed
Must rebuild all associations when re-adding panel
Panel Management
AC225IP Replacement:
Can only replace A-series panel with PCBA board, not complete unit
NG cannot swap B-series for A-series panels - requires database rebuild
Pro allows mixing A and B daughterboards - no differentiation in database
Pro panels can be swapped (B for A) with just IP reconfiguration
NDA Process for API/SDK Access
Requirements:
All API/SDK access requires signed NDA
Customer must complete comprehensive NDA request form
Form requires company registration number, tax ID, and detailed information
Process managed through Boris (sales), previously through legal department
Workflow:
Customer requests API/SDK access
Technical support sends NDA request form to customer
Customer completes and returns form
Form forwarded to Boris (or designated contact)
Israel generates NDA with customer information
Customer electronically signs
Mickey provides API access directly to customer
API Support:
Mickey provides all API support - not handled by technical support
Documentation available at RossLairSecurity.stoplight.io
API allows most functionality available in client application
Increased volume of NDA requests in recent year(s) - went from 1-2/year to 4-5 simultaneously
Known Integrations:
NEXLAR (Houston) built web front-end using NG SDK
Elevator Control Implementation
Standard Configuration:
Each relay assigned to one elevator button
One panel required per elevator cab
Relay board required per cab
AC215 panels support up
